stem: The prefix RE- means again or back. Based on this, REPLAY most nearly means:
explanation: REPLAY decomposes into RE- (again, back) plus PLAY. The prefix RE- appears in REWIND, REVISIT, and REBUILD, all signaling repetition or return. Choice A inverts the meaning by swapping RE- for PRE- (before). Choice B drops the prefix entirely and defines only the root. Choice D confuses RE- with the negating prefix REF- in REFUSE, which carries a different Latin origin (re- plus fundere, to pour back). The correct reading combines repetition with the action of playing.

stem: The prefix NON- means not or absence of. Based on this, NONFICTION most nearly means:
choices: writing that is not made up
explanation: NONFICTION breaks into NON- (not) plus FICTION (invented narrative). The prefix NON- works the same way in NONSTOP, NONPROFIT, NONVERBAL, and NONSENSE, each simply negating the root that follows. Choice A defines fiction itself and ignores the prefix entirely, the most common error when the test taker rushes past the leading morpheme. Choice C confuses NON- with the Latin MAL- (bad) seen in MALPRACTICE and MALFUNCTION, which carries a moral judgment NON- does not. Choice D confuses NON- with the suffix -LESS or with the prefix UN- as in UNFINISHED. NONFICTION names writing grounded in fact rather than invention.

stem: The prefix DIS- means not, opposite of, or away. Based on this, DISLOYAL most nearly means:
explanation: DISLOYAL splits into DIS- (not, opposite of) plus LOYAL. The same prefix appears in DISAGREE, DISHONEST, and DISCONNECT, each reversing the root. Choice A inverts the meaning by treating DIS- as an intensifier, a common error since spoken stress can sound emphatic. Choice B softens the negation into a partial state the prefix does not allow. Choice D ignores the prefix and defines only the root. DISLOYAL describes someone who fails to keep faith with a person, cause, or country.

stem: The prefix TRANS- means across or beyond. The word TRANSPORT therefore is best defined as:
choices: to carry across or from one place to another
to load goods onto a ship
explanation: TRANSPORT combines TRANS- (across) with the Latin root PORT (to carry), the same root in PORTER, IMPORT, and EXPORT. The prefix appears in TRANSMIT, TRANSFER, and TRANSCONTINENTAL with consistent meaning. Choice B mistakes PORT the prefix-bearing root for PORT meaning harbor, a homograph trap. Choice C invents a meaning unsupported by either morpheme. Choice D names a specific shipping action but ignores the across or beyond element supplied by TRANS-. TRANSPORT denotes movement of people or goods across distance.

stem: The suffix -LESS means without. Based on this, FEARLESS most nearly means:
explanation: FEARLESS attaches the suffix -LESS (without) to the root FEAR. The same suffix produces HOPELESS, ENDLESS, and HARMLESS, each signaling absence of the root quality. Choice A reverses the meaning, the kind of error that arises when a reader confuses -LESS with -FUL. Choice B softens the absolute negation the suffix demands. Choice C confuses -LESS with the agentive suffix -SOME as in FEARSOME, which means causing fear. FEARLESS describes a person or action free from fear.

stem: The suffix -FUL means full of or characterized by. The word PLAYFUL therefore most nearly means:
choices: full of or showing a desire to play
explanation: PLAYFUL joins the root PLAY with the suffix -FUL (full of). The same suffix produces JOYFUL, CAREFUL, and HOPEFUL, each meaning full of the root quality. Choice B reverses the meaning by treating -FUL as if it were -LESS. Choice C drifts toward modern colloquial English where playful is sometimes loosely linked to exhaustion, but morphology does not support that reading. Choice D narrows the word to athletic skill, ignoring the broad sense of disposition the suffix supplies. PLAYFUL describes a mood or behavior marked by lighthearted fun.

stem: The prefix INTER- means between or among. Based on this, INTERSTATE most nearly means:
explanation: INTERSTATE pairs INTER- (between, among) with STATE. The same prefix carries through INTERNATIONAL, INTERACT, INTERSECT, and INTERVIEW, each describing something that occurs between or among multiple parties. Choice A confuses INTER- with INTRA- (within), a high-frequency trap since the two prefixes differ by a single letter but reverse the meaning, as in INTRASTATE commerce that stays within one state. Choice C ignores the prefix entirely and assigns government ownership unsupported by the morphology. Choice D confuses INTER- with SUB- (below) or with the suffix -ARY indicating subordinate rank. An INTERSTATE highway runs between states, which is why the federal system carries that label.

stem: The prefix MAL- means bad or wrongful. The word MALCONTENT therefore is best defined as:
choices: a person who is dissatisfied or rebellious
explanation: MALCONTENT joins MAL- (bad, wrongful) with CONTENT (satisfied). The same prefix drives MALFUNCTION, MALPRACTICE, MALEVOLENT, and MALICE, each signaling a corrupt or harmful version of the root. Choice A defines CONTENT alone and ignores the prefix entirely, the most common slip when a reader recognizes only the familiar second morpheme. Choice B confuses MAL- with the prefix DIS- (apart, away) or with the idea of withdrawal, but MAL- carries no sense of avoidance. Choice D drifts into a vague personality description unsupported by either morpheme. A MALCONTENT is a person whose dissatisfaction often expresses itself as grumbling or open rebellion against authority.

stem: The suffix -ISM means a doctrine, belief, or practice. Based on this, BAPTISM most nearly means:
choices: the person who performs a religious rite
the practice or rite of baptizing
a building used for religious services
explanation: BAPTISM combines the Greek root BAPT (to dip, immerse) with the suffix -ISM (practice, doctrine). The same suffix produces CAPITALISM, JOURNALISM, and CRITICISM, each naming a practice or system. Choice A confuses -ISM with the agentive suffix -IST, which names the person who practices (BAPTIST). Choice C confuses -ISM with -ARY or -ERY (BAPTISTRY, the building). Choice D confuses -ISM with -OLOGY (study of). BAPTISM names the rite itself rather than its practitioner, location, or academic study.

stem: The word AMBIDEXTROUS contains the prefix AMBI- (both) and the root DEXTR- (right hand, skill). AMBIDEXTROUS most nearly means:
choices: unable to use either hand well
able to use both hands with equal skill
explanation: AMBIDEXTROUS decomposes into AMBI- (both) plus DEXTR- (right hand, skill) plus the adjective suffix -OUS. The same prefix appears in AMBIGUOUS (both ways) and AMBIVALENT (both feelings). Choice A inverts the meaning by stripping the positive sense of DEXTR-. Choice C confuses DEXTR- with SINISTR- (left hand), the Latin opposite preserved in SINISTER. Choice D ignores both morphemes and describes a generic lack of physical skill. AMBIDEXTROUS describes a person who handles tools, writing, or tasks equally well with either hand.

stem: The word MISANTHROPE contains the Greek prefix MIS- (hatred of) and the root ANTHROP- (humankind). MISANTHROPE most nearly means:
choices: a person who studies human cultures
a person who hates or distrusts humankind
a person who acts wrongly toward others
a person who lives among other people
explanation: MISANTHROPE comes from Greek MIS- (hatred) plus ANTHROP- (man, humankind), the same root in ANTHROPOLOGY and PHILANTHROPY. The trap is that Greek MIS- (hatred, as in MISOGYNY) is spelled identically to the Latin MIS- (wrongly, as in MISJUDGE), but the two prefixes are unrelated. Choice A confuses MISANTHROPE with ANTHROPOLOGIST, swapping the prefix for the suffix -OLOGIST. Choice C applies the Latin MIS- (wrongly) instead of the Greek one. Choice D ignores the hostility carried by MIS- entirely. A MISANTHROPE actively dislikes the human race.

stem: The word BIBLIOPHILE contains the root BIBLIO- (book) and the suffix -PHILE (lover of). BIBLIOPHILE most nearly means:
choices: one who loves and collects books
explanation: BIBLIOPHILE pairs BIBLIO- (book) with -PHILE (lover of), the same suffix in AUDIOPHILE and FRANCOPHILE. The trap is the closely spelled -PHOBE (fear of), which reverses the meaning, as in CLAUSTROPHOBE. Choice A confuses -PHILE with the agentive ending in AUTHOR. Choice B applies -PHOBE instead of -PHILE, a high-value distractor because the suffixes look almost identical to a quick reader. Choice D narrows the word to commercial activity not supported by either morpheme. A BIBLIOPHILE is a devoted reader and collector of books.

stem: The suffix -EFY (a variant of -IFY) means to make or cause to become. The word LIQUEFY therefore is best defined as:
choices: to study the properties of liquid
explanation: LIQUEFY joins LIQU- (the root in LIQUID) with -EFY (to make), a rare variant of -IFY preserved in only a handful of words such as PUTREFY and STUPEFY. Most English verbs use -IFY (CLARIFY, MAGNIFY), so spellers often miswrite LIQUIFY, but the dictionary form keeps the older -EFY. Choice A inverts the meaning by treating -EFY as if it carried negation. Choice C confuses -EFY (to make) with the prefix DE- (to remove). Choice D confuses -EFY with -OLOGY (study of). LIQUEFY names the process of melting or dissolving a solid into liquid form.

stem: The word EPIDERMIS contains the Greek prefix EPI- (upon, over) and the root DERM- (skin). EPIDERMIS most nearly means:
explanation: EPIDERMIS combines EPI- (upon, over) with DERM- (skin), the same root in DERMATOLOGY. The trap is the prefix APO- (away from), which looks and sounds similar to EPI- but reverses the spatial sense, as in APOSTASY. Choice A confuses EPI- with HYPO- (under), the prefix found in HYPODERMIC (under the skin). Choice C also applies HYPO- or SUB- instead of EPI-. Choice D confuses the prefix with the suffix -ITIS (inflammation) or -OSIS (condition). EPIDERMIS names the outermost protective layer of skin covering the body.
